quiz_questions.question_text: Which state of matter has a definite shape and a definite volume?
quiz_questions.explanation: Solids have particles tightly packed in fixed positions, giving them both a definite shape and a definite volume. Liquids have definite volume but not shape; gases have neither.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which state of matter has no definite shape and no definite volume?
quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles are far apart and move freely, so a gas will expand to fill whatever container it occupies — it has neither a definite shape nor a definite volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which state of matter can particles slide past one another while staying close together?
quiz_questions.explanation: Liquid particles are close together (giving liquids a definite volume) but can move past each other, which is why liquids flow and take the shape of their container.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the fourth state of matter, commonly found in stars and lightning?
quiz_questions.explanation: Plasma is an ionized gas made of free-moving electrons and ions. It is created at extremely high temperatures and is the most abundant state of matter in the universe (stars, lightning, neon signs).

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to particle motion when the temperature of a substance increases?
quiz_questions.explanation: Temperature is a measure of average kinetic energy. When temperature increases, particles gain energy and move faster and more vigorously.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which state of matter has particles that vibrate in place but do not move freely?
quiz_questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are locked in fixed positions and can only vibrate. They do not move freely like liquid or gas particles. This is why solids maintain a definite shape.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which state of matter has the highest average kinetic energy at a given temperature?
quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles move the fastest and most freely, giving them the highest kinetic energy among the three common states. Solids have the lowest kinetic energy because their particles only vibrate in place.

quiz_questions.question_text: A gas will expand to fill its container because its particles are:

options.text: Far apart and moving at high speeds
Close together and sliding past each other

quiz_questions.explanation: Gas particles are widely spaced and move rapidly in all directions. Because of this, they spread out to occupy the entire volume of any container they are placed in.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which state of matter is the most common in the visible universe?
quiz_questions.explanation: Plasma makes up over 99% of the visible universe. Stars, including our Sun, are made of plasma. On Earth, plasma is less common but appears in lightning, neon signs, and fluorescent lights.

quiz_questions.question_text: Liquids have a definite _____ but not a definite _____.
quiz_questions.explanation: Liquid particles are close enough together to maintain a fixed volume, but they can slide past each other, so a liquid takes the shape of whatever container it is in.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'solid' in chemistry?

options.text: Matter with a definite shape and definite volume whose particles vibrate in fixed positions
Matter that flows freely and has no definite shape
A substance that always dissolves in water
Any material that is hard to the touch

quiz_questions.explanation: A solid is a state of matter where particles are tightly packed in fixed, ordered positions. They vibrate but do not translate freely, giving solids both a definite shape and a definite volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'kinetic energy' mean in the context of states of matter?

options.text: The energy of particle motion — faster particles have more kinetic energy
The energy stored in chemical bonds
The energy required to break a molecule apart
The total mass of all particles in a sample

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy (KE = ½mv²) is the energy a particle possesses due to its motion. In the context of states of matter, higher kinetic energy means particles move faster, which is directly related to higher temperature.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'plasma'?

options.text: An ionized gas of free electrons and ions formed at very high temperatures
A very cold gas found in deep space
A liquid with extremely low viscosity

quiz_questions.explanation: Plasma is the fourth state of matter, formed when a gas is heated to such high temperatures that atoms become ionized — electrons are stripped away, creating a mix of free electrons and positive ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'phase' mean in chemistry?

options.text: A physically distinct form of matter — solid, liquid, gas, or plasma
A step in a chemical reaction
The speed at which particles move

quiz_questions.explanation: A phase is a distinct form of matter with uniform physical properties. The most common phases are solid, liquid, gas, and plasma. Phase changes occur when energy is added or removed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'viscosity'?

options.text: A liquid's resistance to flow, caused by intermolecular forces between particles
The temperature at which a liquid boils
The amount of gas dissolved in a liquid
The density of a solid material

quiz_questions.explanation: Viscosity measures how much a fluid resists flowing. Honey has high viscosity (flows slowly) while water has low viscosity (flows easily). Stronger intermolecular forces lead to higher viscosity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'intermolecular forces'?

options.text: Attractive forces between molecules that influence a substance's physical properties and state
Forces inside the nucleus of an atom
The bonds between atoms within a single molecule

quiz_questions.explanation: Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractions between neighboring molecules. They determine melting point, boiling point, viscosity, and which state of matter a substance exists in at a given temperature. Stronger IMFs mean higher melting/boiling points.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'thermal energy'?

options.text: The total kinetic energy of all particles in a substance
The potential energy stored in chemical bonds
The energy released during a nuclear reaction
The energy needed to change an object's shape

quiz_questions.explanation: Thermal energy is the sum of the kinetic energies of all particles in a substance. It increases with temperature and with the amount of matter in the sample.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'surface tension' mean?

options.text: The tendency of a liquid's surface to resist being stretched or broken, due to intermolecular forces
The pressure exerted by a gas on container walls
The hardness of a solid surface
The rate at which a liquid evaporates

quiz_questions.explanation: Surface tension is caused by the net inward pull of intermolecular forces on molecules at a liquid's surface. This creates a 'skin-like' barrier that allows small objects (like water striders) to rest on the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'absolute zero'?

options.text: The lowest possible temperature (0 K or −273.15°C) where particle motion theoretically stops
The temperature at which water freezes (0°C)
The temperature at which all matter becomes plasma
The average temperature in outer space

quiz_questions.explanation: Absolute zero (0 Kelvin, −273.15°C) is the theoretical lower limit of temperature. At this point, particles would have minimum possible energy and essentially no thermal motion. It has never been fully reached in practice.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'amorphous solid' mean?

options.text: A solid that lacks an ordered, repeating crystalline structure
A solid that easily dissolves in water
A solid with a very high melting point
A solid that is always transparent

quiz_questions.explanation: Amorphous solids (like glass, rubber, and some plastics) lack the long-range ordered arrangement of particles found in crystalline solids. They soften gradually over a range of temperatures rather than melting at a sharp melting point.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student leaves an ice cube on the kitchen counter. After 20 minutes, there is a puddle of water. Which states of matter are demonstrated in this observation?

options.text: Solid changing to liquid — the ice melted as it absorbed thermal energy from the room
Gas changing to liquid — water vapor condensed on the counter
Liquid changing to gas — the water evaporated quickly
Plasma changing to solid — the ice was formed from plasma

quiz_questions.explanation: The ice cube (solid) absorbed heat energy from the warmer room. Once enough energy was gained to overcome the intermolecular forces holding the solid structure, the particles began moving more freely, producing liquid water.

quiz_questions.question_text: A helium balloon rises into the sky and eventually pops. As it rose, the balloon got larger. Which property of gases explains why the balloon expanded?

options.text: Gas particles spread out to fill available space — lower atmospheric pressure at altitude allows expansion
The helium changed from gas to plasma at high altitude
The rubber of the balloon melted in the sunlight
Gravity compressed the gas, making the balloon wider

quiz_questions.explanation: Gases have no definite volume and will expand to equalize pressure. As the balloon rose, atmospheric pressure decreased, so the helium inside pushed outward more strongly than the outside air pushed in, causing the balloon to expand until it popped.

quiz_questions.question_text: A welder's arc produces temperatures above 10,000°C and a bright glowing stream. What state of matter is most likely present at the tip of the welding arc?

options.text: Plasma — the extreme temperature ionizes atoms into free electrons and ions
Gas — the metal simply evaporates at high temperature
Liquid — the metal melts and glows red
Solid — the metal stays solid but emits light

quiz_questions.explanation: At temperatures above 10,000°C, atoms gain enough energy to ionize — electrons are stripped from their atoms, creating plasma. The bright glow comes from ions and electrons recombining and emitting photons of light.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sealed syringe filled with air is compressed by pushing the plunger in. The volume decreases but no air escapes. Which property of gases allows this compression?

options.text: Gas particles are far apart with empty space between them, allowing them to be pushed closer together
Gas particles are tightly packed and slide past each other
The air converts to a liquid under pressure
The syringe walls absorb the gas molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: Unlike solids and liquids, gas particles have large amounts of empty space between them. When pressure is applied, the particles can be forced closer together, reducing the volume. This is why gases are compressible while liquids and solids are essentially incompressible.

quiz_questions.question_text: Dry ice (solid CO₂) is placed on a table and produces white fog while shrinking, but no puddle forms underneath. What process is occurring?

options.text: Sublimation — the solid CO₂ is converting directly to gas without becoming a liquid
Evaporation — the liquid CO₂ is turning into gas too fast to see
Condensation — the CO₂ gas is turning back into a solid
Deposition — the gas is forming ice crystals

quiz_questions.explanation: At normal atmospheric pressure, CO₂ goes directly from solid to gas (sublimation) without passing through the liquid phase. The white 'fog' is actually water vapor from the surrounding air condensing due to the extreme cold of the dry ice.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chef notices that honey flows much more slowly than water when poured from a spoon. Both are liquids, so what property explains why honey flows more slowly?

options.text: Viscosity — honey has stronger intermolecular forces, making it more resistant to flow
Density — honey is denser so gravity pulls it down faster
Surface tension — honey has a stronger surface skin
Temperature — honey is always colder than water

quiz_questions.explanation: Viscosity is a liquid's resistance to flow. Honey has strong intermolecular forces between its sugar molecules, making it highly viscous. Water has weaker intermolecular forces and therefore lower viscosity. Warming honey reduces its viscosity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A camper notices that water boils at about 95°C at their mountain campsite instead of the usual 100°C at sea level. What causes this difference?

options.text: Lower atmospheric pressure at high altitude means less energy is needed for particles to escape as gas
Mountain water contains different minerals that lower the boiling point
The campfire produces less heat at high altitude
Cold mountain air cools the water faster so it boils sooner

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling occurs when a liquid's vapor pressure equals the atmospheric pressure above it. At higher altitude, atmospheric pressure is lower, so less energy (lower temperature) is needed for water molecules to escape into the gas phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist places an iron block, a wooden block, and a sealed balloon in a box. She compresses only the balloon significantly. Why can the gas be compressed but the solid objects cannot?

options.text: Gas particles have large spaces between them that can be reduced; solid particles are already packed closely together
Solids are heavier than gases so they resist compression
The balloon is made of rubber, which is more flexible than iron or wood
Gas particles are smaller than solid particles

quiz_questions.explanation: Compressibility depends on the spacing between particles, not their size or weight. Gas particles are widely spaced with mostly empty space, so they can be pushed closer together. Solid (and liquid) particles are already close, leaving almost no room for compression.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neon signs glow with bright colors when electricity passes through the gas inside the tube. What state of matter produces the characteristic glow?

options.text: Plasma — electricity ionizes the neon gas, and ions emit light when electrons return to lower energy levels
Gas — neon gas naturally glows at room temperature
Liquid — melted neon produces light when heated
Solid — solid neon crystals emit light when struck by electricity

quiz_questions.explanation: When high-voltage electricity passes through a sealed tube of neon gas, it ionizes the atoms — stripping electrons and creating plasma. As the excited electrons fall back to lower energy levels, they emit photons of visible light, producing the characteristic neon glow.

quiz_questions.question_text: A forensic scientist discovers an unknown clear liquid at a crime scene. She measures its boiling point as 78.37°C and its density as 0.789 g/mL, which match known values for ethanol. Why can these two measurements identify the substance?

options.text: Boiling point and density are intensive properties — they are characteristic of a substance regardless of sample size
Every liquid has a unique color that corresponds to its boiling point
Mass and volume always identify a substance
The amount of liquid determines its boiling point

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling point and density are intensive properties — they do not change with the amount of substance. Each pure substance has unique intensive property values that act like a fingerprint. Mass and volume alone (extensive properties) change with sample size and cannot identify a substance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the phase change from solid to liquid called?
quiz_questions.explanation: Melting is the transition from solid to liquid. It occurs when a solid absorbs enough energy to overcome the forces holding its particles in fixed positions, allowing them to move more freely.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the phase change from gas to liquid called?
quiz_questions.explanation: Condensation occurs when gas particles lose energy and slow down enough for intermolecular forces to pull them into the closer arrangement of a liquid. It is an exothermic process (releases energy).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which phase change goes directly from solid to gas without passing through the liquid phase?
quiz_questions.explanation: Sublimation is the direct transition from solid to gas. A common example is dry ice (solid CO₂), which turns directly into carbon dioxide gas at normal atmospheric pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a phase change, what happens to the temperature of the substance?
quiz_questions.explanation: During a phase change, all added or removed energy goes toward breaking or forming intermolecular forces — not changing temperature. This is why a heating curve shows flat plateaus at the melting and boiling points.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which phase change releases energy to the surroundings?
quiz_questions.explanation: Freezing (liquid → solid) is exothermic — particles slow down and form stronger intermolecular bonds, releasing energy. Melting, evaporation, and sublimation all absorb energy (endothermic).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the phase change from liquid to gas called?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vaporization is the transition from liquid to gas. It can occur as evaporation (at the surface, below boiling point) or boiling (throughout the liquid, at the boiling point). Both are endothermic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is deposition?

options.text: Gas changing directly to solid without becoming liquid first

quiz_questions.explanation: Deposition is the reverse of sublimation — gas converts directly to solid without passing through the liquid phase. Frost forming on a cold window is a common example. It is an exothermic process.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which phase change requires the most energy input: melting or evaporation?

options.text: Evaporation — because all intermolecular forces must be overcome for particles to become gas
Melting — because solid particles are harder to separate than liquid particles
They require exactly the same amount of energy
Neither requires energy — both are exothermic

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation (or boiling) requires more energy than melting because particles must completely overcome all intermolecular forces to enter the gas phase. In melting, particles only loosen their fixed positions while staying close together.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between evaporation and boiling?

options.text: Evaporation occurs at the surface below the boiling point; boiling occurs throughout the liquid at the boiling point
Evaporation produces steam; boiling does not
Boiling only happens in water; evaporation happens in all liquids
There is no difference — they are the same process

quiz_questions.explanation: Both are forms of vaporization (liquid → gas), but evaporation occurs only at the liquid surface and can happen at any temperature. Boiling occurs throughout the entire liquid when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure at the boiling point.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a heating curve, flat horizontal sections (plateaus) represent:

options.text: Phase changes — energy is used to break intermolecular forces, not raise temperature
Periods when no heat is being added
Temperature increases within a single phase

quiz_questions.explanation: During a phase change, the temperature stays constant even though heat is being added. All the energy goes into breaking intermolecular forces (changing the arrangement of particles) rather than increasing kinetic energy (temperature).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'melting point'?

options.text: The temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid at standard pressure
The temperature at which a liquid changes to a gas
The temperature at which a substance decomposes
The temperature at which a gas becomes plasma

quiz_questions.explanation: The melting point is the specific temperature at which a solid's particles gain enough energy to break free from their fixed positions and transition to the liquid phase. At standard pressure, ice melts at 0°C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'endothermic' mean in the context of phase changes?

options.text: A process that absorbs energy from the surroundings
A process that releases energy to the surroundings
A process that creates new chemical bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Endothermic means 'heat in' — the process absorbs energy from the surroundings. Melting, evaporation, and sublimation are all endothermic because energy must be added to overcome intermolecular forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'exothermic' mean in the context of phase changes?

options.text: A process that releases energy to the surroundings
A process that absorbs energy from the surroundings
A process that only occurs at high temperatures
A process that breaks chemical bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Exothermic means 'heat out' — the process releases energy to the surroundings. Freezing, condensation, and deposition are exothermic because particles form stronger intermolecular bonds and release the stored energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'heat of fusion'?

options.text: The energy required to change a substance from solid to liquid at its melting point
The energy required to change a substance from liquid to gas
The temperature at which two metals fuse together
The total thermal energy in a solid object

quiz_questions.explanation: Heat of fusion (ΔHfus) is the amount of energy needed per gram (or mole) to convert a solid to a liquid at its melting point. For water, this is 334 J/g. The reverse process (freezing) releases the same amount of energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'heat of vaporization'?

options.text: The energy required to change a substance from liquid to gas at its boiling point
The energy required to change a substance from solid to liquid
The temperature at which a liquid begins to boil
The speed at which gas particles move

quiz_questions.explanation: Heat of vaporization (ΔHvap) is the energy needed per gram (or mole) to convert a liquid to gas at its boiling point. For water, it is 2,260 J/g — much larger than the heat of fusion because all intermolecular forces must be fully overcome.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'boiling point' mean?

options.text: The temperature at which a liquid's vapor pressure equals the atmospheric pressure above it
The temperature at which a solid melts
The highest temperature a liquid can reach
The temperature at which bubbles first appear in any liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling point is the temperature at which the vapor pressure of a liquid equals the external atmospheric pressure, causing vaporization throughout the liquid (not just at the surface). At sea level, water boils at 100°C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'heating curve'?

options.text: A graph showing temperature vs. energy added, with plateaus at phase changes
A measurement of how fast a substance heats up
A device used to heat liquids in a lab
A table listing boiling points of all elements

quiz_questions.explanation: A heating curve plots temperature on the y-axis against energy (or time of heating) on the x-axis. Sloped sections show temperature rising within a single phase; flat plateaus show phase changes where temperature stays constant as energy goes into breaking intermolecular forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'vapor pressure' mean?

options.text: The pressure exerted by the vapor above a liquid in a closed container at equilibrium
The weight of water vapor in the atmosphere
The force needed to compress a gas into a liquid
The temperature at which vapor turns back into liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by a vapor in equilibrium with its liquid (or solid) in a closed container. It increases with temperature because more molecules have enough energy to escape into the gas phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'triple point' of a substance?

options.text: The specific temperature and pressure at which solid, liquid, and gas phases coexist in equilibrium
The point at which three different substances mix together
The temperature at which a substance boils three times faster
The third phase change in a heating curve

quiz_questions.explanation: The triple point is a unique set of temperature and pressure conditions where all three phases of a substance exist simultaneously in equilibrium. For water, the triple point is 0.01°C at 611.73 Pa.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'condensation point' mean?

options.text: The temperature at which a gas changes to a liquid — numerically equal to the boiling point
The pressure at which a gas becomes a solid
The point at which a liquid becomes saturated
The temperature at which dew forms, which varies based on humidity

quiz_questions.explanation: The condensation point and the boiling point are the same temperature for a given substance at the same pressure. The process is just reversed: boiling adds energy (endothermic), while condensation releases energy (exothermic).

quiz_questions.question_text: On a cold morning, a student sees frost forming on a car windshield even though it did not rain overnight. Which phase change produced the frost?

options.text: Deposition — water vapor in the air changed directly to solid ice on the cold surface
Freezing — liquid water on the windshield froze overnight
Condensation — water vapor became liquid droplets that then froze
Sublimation — ice from the ground rose up to the windshield

quiz_questions.explanation: Frost forms through deposition — water vapor (gas) in the air contacts the cold windshield surface and transitions directly to solid ice crystals without first becoming liquid. This is the reverse of sublimation and is exothermic.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pot of water is boiling on a stove. A student measures the temperature and finds it stays at 100°C even as more heat is added. Why doesn't the temperature increase?

options.text: During the phase change, all added energy goes into breaking intermolecular forces rather than raising temperature
The thermometer is broken and cannot read above 100°C
The stove stopped producing heat once the water reached 100°C
Water cannot absorb any more energy after reaching 100°C

quiz_questions.explanation: At the boiling point, energy is used to overcome intermolecular forces and convert liquid molecules to gas — this is the heat of vaporization. Temperature only rises again once all the liquid has been converted to steam.

quiz_questions.question_text: Wet clothes hung on a clothesline dry even on a cool day when the temperature is well below 100°C. What process is responsible?

options.text: Evaporation — surface water molecules with enough kinetic energy escape into the air
Boiling — the water boils at lower temperatures outdoors
Sublimation — the water turns directly from liquid to solid
Condensation — moisture leaves the clothes and enters the air as liquid droplets

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation occurs at any temperature when surface molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome intermolecular forces and escape into the gas phase. Wind and low humidity speed up evaporation by removing vapor from the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hiker at high altitude (5,000 m) tries to cook pasta but notices the water boils at about 83°C instead of 100°C. The pasta takes much longer to cook. Why?

options.text: Lower atmospheric pressure lowers the boiling point, so the water is less hot and cooks food more slowly
The water contains fewer minerals at high altitude
UV radiation at high altitude prevents water from reaching 100°C
The thinner air makes the flame on the stove weaker

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling occurs when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure. At 5,000 m, atmospheric pressure is much lower, so water boils at ~83°C. Since the water is cooler, chemical reactions in cooking (which depend on temperature) proceed more slowly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places mothballs in a closet. Over several weeks, the mothballs shrink and eventually disappear, but no liquid residue is found. What phase change occurred?

options.text: Sublimation — the solid naphthalene changed directly to gas
Evaporation — the mothballs melted and then evaporated
Decomposition — the mothballs broke down into elements
Condensation — the mothball gas became liquid and drained away

quiz_questions.explanation: Mothballs are made of naphthalene (or paradichlorobenzene), which sublimes at room temperature — the solid slowly converts directly to gas without becoming liquid. This is why you can smell them (the gas reaches your nose) but never see a puddle.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a humid summer day, water droplets form on the outside of a cold glass of iced tea. Which phase change causes these droplets to appear?

options.text: Condensation — water vapor in the warm air cools on the cold glass surface and becomes liquid
Melting — ice from inside the glass leaks through
Deposition — water vapor freezes directly onto the glass
Evaporation — the tea inside evaporates through the glass

quiz_questions.explanation: Warm, humid air contains water vapor. When this air contacts the cold glass surface, the vapor molecules lose kinetic energy and slow down enough for intermolecular forces to pull them together into liquid droplets — this is condensation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pressure cooker seals steam inside, raising the internal pressure above normal atmospheric pressure. How does this affect the boiling point of the water inside?

options.text: The boiling point increases — higher pressure requires more energy for molecules to escape into the gas phase
The boiling point decreases — pressure forces water to boil faster
The boiling point stays the same — pressure does not affect phase changes
The water cannot boil at all under high pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Increased pressure raises the boiling point because more energy is needed for liquid molecules to push against the higher external pressure and escape into the gas phase. A pressure cooker reaches ~120°C, cooking food faster than normal boiling.

quiz_questions.question_text: After stepping out of a swimming pool, a person feels cold even though the air temperature is 30°C. Which phase change is responsible for the cold sensation?

options.text: Evaporation — water on the skin absorbs body heat as it changes from liquid to gas
Condensation — the air condenses moisture onto the cold skin
Freezing — the pool water freezes on the skin surface
Sublimation — the water skips the liquid phase and becomes gas

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporation is endothermic — it absorbs heat energy from the surroundings (your skin). As water molecules on your skin gain enough energy to escape into the gas phase, they take that thermal energy with them, leaving your skin feeling cold.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist examines a phase diagram and notices that CO₂ has no liquid phase at normal atmospheric pressure (1 atm). What does this tell us about dry ice at room conditions?

options.text: At 1 atm, solid CO₂ can only sublime — it cannot melt into a liquid
CO₂ has no solid phase at any pressure
Liquid CO₂ exists at 1 atm but evaporates too quickly to see
CO₂ goes directly from gas to plasma at room temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: The phase diagram for CO₂ shows that the triple point is at 5.11 atm. Below this pressure, the liquid phase does not exist, so solid CO₂ at 1 atm can only sublimate directly to gas. Liquid CO₂ requires pressures above 5.11 atm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A freeze-drying machine first freezes food solid, then lowers the pressure and gently heats it so the ice disappears without the food ever becoming wet. Which sequence of phase changes makes this possible?

options.text: Freezing (liquid → solid), then sublimation (solid → gas) under low pressure
Freezing (liquid → solid), then melting (solid → liquid), then evaporation (liquid → gas)

quiz_questions.explanation: Freeze-drying works by first freezing the food, then reducing pressure so the frozen water sublimes directly from solid to gas — bypassing the liquid phase entirely. This preserves the food's structure because it never becomes wet or soggy during the drying process.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is an intensive property?
quiz_questions.explanation: Density does not depend on the amount of matter — a small piece of gold has the same density as a large bar. Mass, volume, and length all change when the sample size changes, making them extensive properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is an extensive property?
quiz_questions.explanation: Mass depends on the amount of matter — doubling the sample doubles the mass. Boiling point, color, and density stay the same regardless of sample size, so they are intensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: Intensive properties do NOT depend on:

options.text: The amount of matter in the sample
The chemical composition of the substance

quiz_questions.explanation: Intensive properties are independent of ammount — a cup of water and an ocean of water have the same boiling point, density, and color. Extensive properties (mass, volume) change with sample size.

quiz_questions.question_text: Boiling point is classified as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling point does not change with sample size — water boils at 100°C whether you have 10 mL or 10 L. This makes it an intensive property, useful for identifying substances.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you cut a block of iron in half, which property changes?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cutting the block in half reduces the amount of matter, so mass and volume (extensive properties) decrease. Density, melting point, and color remain unchanged because they are intensive properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Temperature is classified as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles, not the total — it doesn't change when you combine or divide a sample (at the same conditions). This makes it intensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which pair are both extensive properties?
quiz_questions.explanation: Mass and volume both depend on the amount of substance — doubling the sample doubles both. Density, color, temperature, pressure, melting point, and hardness are all intensive properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which property can be used to identify an unknown substance?

options.text: Density — because it is the same regardless of sample size
Mass — because heavier substances are easier to identify
Volume — because larger samples are more visible
Weight — because each substance has a unique weight

quiz_questions.explanation: Intensive properties like density, melting point, and boiling point are characteristic of a substance and do not change with amount. This makes them useful 'fingerprints' for identification. Mass and volume change with sample size.

quiz_questions.question_text: Color is classified as what type of property?

options.text: Intensive — it does not change with sample size
Extensive — larger samples appear more colorful
Neither — color is not a measurable property
Both — it depends on the substance

quiz_questions.explanation: A small chip of copper and a large copper bar both have the same reddish-orange color. Color does not change with the amount of substance, making it an intensive property.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to extensive properties when you combine two identical samples?

options.text: They double — because they depend on the amount of matter
They stay the same — properties never change

quiz_questions.explanation: Extensive properties are additive. If you combine two 50 g samples of water, the total mass is 100 g and the total volume doubles. Intensive properties like temperature and density remain unchanged upon mixing identical samples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of an 'intensive property'?

options.text: A property that does NOT depend on the amount of matter in the sample
A property that increases when more matter is added
A property that can only be measured in a laboratory
A property that requires intense energy to observe

quiz_questions.explanation: An intensive property is independent of sample size. Examples include density, boiling point, melting point, color, conductivity, and hardness. These are the same whether you have a gram or a kilogram of a substance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of an 'extensive property'?

options.text: A property that DOES depend on the amount of matter in the sample
A property that stays the same regardless of sample size
A property that extends over a large area
A property that is extensively studied in chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: An extensive property changes proportionally with the amount of matter. Mass, volume, length, weight, and total energy are all extensive — double the sample, double the value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'density' and why is it classified as intensive?

options.text: Mass per unit volume (d = m/v); it is intensive because both mass and volume scale equally, so the ratio stays constant
Total mass of a substance; it is intensive because mass never changes
Volume per unit mass; it is intensive because gases always have the same density
Weight divided by temperature; it is intensive because temperature is always the same

quiz_questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume (d = m/v). When you double the amount of substance, both mass and volume double, so their ratio remains unchanged. This makes density an intensive property useful for substance identification.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'melting point' mean, and is it intensive or extensive?

options.text: The temperature at which a solid becomes a liquid; intensive — it does not depend on sample size
The mass at which a solid begins to melt; extensive — larger objects melt at higher temperatures
The amount of energy needed to melt a solid; extensive — more mass requires more energy
The volume change when a solid melts; intensive — volume ratios stay constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Melting point is the temperature at which a solid transitions to liquid. It is the same regardless of sample size (1 g of ice and 1 kg of ice both melt at 0°C), making it an intensive property.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'hardness' and how is it classified?

options.text: A measure of a material's resistance to scratching; it is intensive — a small diamond is just as hard as a large one
The total force needed to break an object; extensive — larger objects are harder to break
The weight of a material; extensive — heavier objects feel harder
The temperature at which a material softens; neither intensive nor extensive

quiz_questions.explanation: Hardness measures how well a material resists being scratched or deformed. It depends on the material's internal structure and bonding, not on how much of it you have, making it an intensive property.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'conductivity' mean, and is it intensive or extensive?

options.text: A material's ability to conduct heat or electricity; intensive — a small copper wire conducts the same per unit as a large one
The total amount of electricity flowing through a wire; extensive — longer wires carry more current
The speed at which heat travels; neither intensive nor extensive
The energy released when a material conducts; extensive — it depends on wire length

quiz_questions.explanation: Conductivity is an intrinsic property of a material describing how well it transfers heat or electrical charge per unit dimensions. Copper has the same conductivity whether it's a wire or a block, making it intensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'luster'?

options.text: The way a material's surface reflects light; an intensive property
The total amount of light emitted by a material; an extensive property
The transparency of a material; neither intensive nor extensive
The brightness of a material when heated; depends on sample size

quiz_questions.explanation: Luster describes how a material's surface appears when it reflects light — metallic, glassy, waxy, dull, etc. A small piece of gold has the same metallic luster as a large piece, making it an intensive property.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'malleability' mean?

options.text: The ability of a material to be hammered into thin sheets without breaking; an intensive property of metals
The total energy needed to flatten a material; an extensive property
The ability to dissolve in water; an intensive property of ionic compounds
The ability to conduct electricity; an extensive property of metals

quiz_questions.explanation: Malleability is the ability of a material to be deformed into sheets by hammering or rolling. Gold is the most malleable metal. This property depends on the material's bonding, not on sample size, making it intensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'specific heat capacity' mean, and is it intensive or extensive?

options.text: The amount of energy needed to raise 1 gram of a substance by 1°C; intensive — it is the same per gram regardless of total mass
The total heat stored in a substance; extensive — larger objects store more heat
The temperature at which a substance absorbs maximum heat; neither
The speed at which a substance heats up; extensive — depends on mass

quiz_questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity (c) is defined per gram — water's specific heat is 4.184 J/(g·°C) whether you have 1 g or 1,000 g. Because it is normalized to mass, it does not change with sample size, making it intensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ductility' mean?

options.text: The ability of a material to be drawn into thin wires without breaking; an intensive property
The hardness of a metallic substance; an extensive property
The tendency of a gas to expand; neither intensive nor extensive
The ability to resist corrosion; both intensive and extensive

quiz_questions.explanation: Ductility is the ability to be stretched into wires. Copper is highly ductile, which is why it is used for electrical wiring. This property depends on the material's bonding, not the amount, making it intensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: A jeweler has a small gold ring and a large gold bar. A customer asks, 'Does the ring have the same density as the bar?' What is the correct answer and why?

options.text: Yes — density is an intensive property and does not change with sample size
No — the bar has more mass, so it must be denser
No — the ring is smaller, so its particles are more tightly packed
It depends on whether the gold is pure or mixed with other metals

quiz_questions.explanation: Density (d = m/v) is intensive — when sample size changes, both mass and volume change proportionally, keeping the ratio constant. Pure gold always has a density of 19.3 g/cm³ regardless of whether it is a ring or a bar.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemistry student measures the boiling point of 50 mL of ethanol and gets 78.4°C. Her lab partner measures the boiling point of 500 mL of ethanol. What result should the partner expect?

options.text: 78.4°C — boiling point is intensive and does not depend on volume
A higher boiling point because there is more liquid to heat
A lower boiling point because the larger sample disperses heat faster
784°C — ten times the volume means ten times the boiling point

quiz_questions.explanation: Boiling point is an intensive property determined by the strength of intermolecular forces in the substance, not the amount. Whether you have 50 mL or 500 mL, pure ethanol boils at 78.4°C at standard pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist needs to identify an unknown metal. She has only a tiny 2 g sample. Her colleague says, 'You need a bigger sample to get accurate property measurements.' Is the colleague correct?

options.text: Not necessarily — intensive properties like density and melting point can identify a substance from any sample size
Yes — small samples have different properties than large ones
Yes — you need at least 100 g to measure any property accurately
No — but only extensive properties can be measured from small samples

quiz_questions.explanation: Intensive properties (density, melting point, color, luster) are the same regardless of how much material you have. A 2 g sample of copper has the same density (8.96 g/cm³) and melting point (1,085°C) as a 2 kg block.

quiz_questions.question_text: A baker doubles a bread recipe — using twice the flour, water, and yeast. Which properties of the dough change, and which stay the same?

options.text: Mass and volume double (extensive); density, color, and texture remain the same (intensive)
All properties double because there is twice as much dough
Only the color changes because there is more flour
No properties change — the recipe stays the same

quiz_questions.explanation: Doubling the recipe doubles the amount of matter, so extensive properties (mass, volume) double. Intensive properties (density, color, texture, taste) remain the same because they depend on what the dough is, not how much you made.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two identical beakers each contain 100 mL of water at 25°C. A student pours them together into one large beaker. What is the temperature of the combined water?

options.text: 25°C — temperature is intensive and does not add up when combining identical samples
12.5°C — the temperature is split between twice the volume
0°C — mixing resets the temperature to baseline

quiz_questions.explanation: Temperature is intensive. When you combine two samples at the same temperature, the result is that same temperature — not the sum. The volume and mass (extensive) will double to 200 mL, but the temperature stays at 25°C.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geologist finds an unknown mineral and records: mass = 45 g, volume = 15 cm³, color = silver-gray, hardness = 6 on Mohs scale. Which measurements are most useful for identifying the mineral?

options.text: Color, hardness, and calculated density (3.0 g/cm³) — all intensive properties characteristic of the mineral
Mass and volume — because bigger minerals are easier to identify
Only mass — it is the most reliable measurement
None of these — you need a chemical analysis to identify any mineral

quiz_questions.explanation: Color, hardness, and density (45 g ÷ 15 cm³ = 3.0 g/cm³) are all intensive properties unique to a substance. These can be compared to known mineral reference values for identification. Mass and volume alone (extensive) would vary with sample size.

quiz_questions.question_text: A shipping company weighs a container of liquid chemical on a scale and records 500 kg. They then split it into ten 50 kg drums. Which property stayed the same, and which changed?

options.text: Density, boiling point (intensive) stayed the same; mass and volume (extensive) of each drum decreased
Everything changed because the liquid is now in different containers
Mass stayed the same in each drum; density decreased
Boiling point decreased because each drum has less liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: Splitting a substance changes its extensive properties (each drum has less mass and volume) but does not change intensive ones (density and boiling point remain identical in each drum because the substance itself hasn't changed).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student argues: 'A lake has more thermal energy than a cup of hot coffee, even though the coffee is hotter.' Is this correct, and which type of property explains each measurement?

options.text: Correct — thermal energy is extensive (depends on total mass), while temperature is intensive (depends on average kinetic energy per particle)
Incorrect — the coffee must have more thermal energy because it is hotter
Incorrect — thermal energy and temperature are both intensive
Correct — but both thermal energy and temperature are extensive

quiz_questions.explanation: Temperature (intensive) measures average kinetic energy per particle. Thermal energy (extensive) is the total kinetic energy of ALL particles. A lake has vastly more particles than a coffee cup, so even at a lower temperature, its total thermal energy is greater.

quiz_questions.question_text: A forensic chemist recovers only a few drops of an unknown liquid from a crime scene. She measures the density as 0.879 g/mL and the boiling point as 56.2°C, matching acetone. Her supervisor asks, 'Can we trust these measurements from such a small sample?' What should she say?

options.text: Yes — density and boiling point are intensive properties, so they are the same regardless of sample size
No — small samples always give inaccurate property measurements
Only the density is reliable; boiling point requires a large sample
No — the small amount of liquid will have a higher density than a larger sample

quiz_questions.explanation: Intensive properties like density and boiling point are intrinsic to the substance itself and do not depend on how much of it you have. Whether you measure a few drops or a full flask, pure acetone has the same density and boiling point.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer designs a heat shield for a spacecraft. She must choose a material based on melting point, thermal conductivity, and density. A colleague suggests, 'We should test a full-size panel, not a small sample, to get accurate values.' Is this necessary?

options.text: No — melting point, thermal conductivity, and density are all intensive properties that can be measured on a small sample
Yes — larger samples give higher melting points
Yes — density only applies to large objects
No — but the engineer should still test extensive properties like total weight

quiz_questions.explanation: Melting point, thermal conductivity, and density are all intensive properties — they characterize the material itself and are the same in a small sample as in a full panel. Testing a small sample is sufficient for measuring these intrinsic properties. Total weight (extensive) would need to be calculated separately for the actual panel size.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for density?
quiz_questions.explanation: Density is calculated as d = m/v (mass divided by volume). Common units include g/mL, g/cm³, and kg/m³. You can rearrange to find mass (m = d·v) or volume (v = m/d).

quiz_questions.question_text: If an object has a mass of 50 g and a volume of 25 mL, what is its density?
quiz_questions.explanation: Density = mass / volume = 50 g / 25 mL = 2 g/mL. The other choices result from multiplying, dividing reversed, or adding the values.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object with a density less than 1 g/mL will _____ in water.
quiz_questions.explanation: Water has a density of approximately 1 g/mL. Objects less dense than the surrounding fluid float; denser objects sink. If an object's density equals the fluid's, it remains neutrally buoyant.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the density of water at standard conditions?
quiz_questions.explanation: Pure water at 4°C has a density of 1.00 g/mL (or equivalently, 1.00 g/cm³ or 1,000 kg/m³). This is a standard reference point for comparing other materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is the volume of an irregularly shaped solid typically measured?

options.text: Water displacement — submerging it and measuring the volume change
Using a ruler to measure length × width × height
Heating it until it melts and measuring the liquid volume

quiz_questions.explanation: For irregular shapes, water displacement is the standard method: fill a graduated cylinder with water, note the level, submerge the object, and read the new level. The difference equals the object's volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: If density remains constant and the mass of a sample doubles, what happens to volume?
quiz_questions.explanation: Since d = m/v, if density is constant and mass doubles, volume must also double to keep the ratio the same: v = m/d, so doubling m doubles v.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which material has the highest density?
quiz_questions.explanation: Lead has a density of about 11.3 g/cm³, far greater than wood (~0.5 g/cm³), cork (~0.12 g/cm³), or air (~0.001 g/cm³). Metals with heavy, tightly packed atoms typically have the highest densities.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the common units for density?
quiz_questions.explanation: Density is mass divided by volume, so its units combine a mass unit over a volume unit: g/mL, g/cm³, or kg/m³. Note that 1 g/mL = 1 g/cm³ because 1 mL = 1 cm³.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is density an intensive or extensive property?

options.text: Intensive — it stays the same regardless of sample size
Extensive — it increases with more matter
Both — it depends on the substance
Neither — density is not a physical property

quiz_questions.explanation: Density is intensive because when you increase the amount of matter, both mass and volume increase equally, keeping the ratio (d = m/v) constant. A gram of gold has the same density as a kilogram of gold.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object sinks in water but floats in mercury (density = 13.6 g/mL). The object's density must be:
quiz_questions.explanation: The object sinks in water (density > 1 g/mL) but floats in mercury (density < 13.6 g/mL). Therefore its density falls between 1 and 13.6 g/mL. For example, iron (7.87 g/cm³) would behave this way.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'density'?

options.text: The mass of a substance per unit volume, expressed as d = m/v
The total volume of a substance
The weight of a substance in air
The number of particles in a given sample

quiz_questions.explanation: Density is defined as mass per unit volume. It describes how tightly matter is packed in a given space. Higher density means more mass concentrated in the same volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'buoyancy' mean?

options.text: The upward force exerted on an object by the fluid it is immersed in, determined by displaced fluid volume
The downward force of gravity on an object in water
The speed at which an object sinks in a liquid
The total mass of water displaced by an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Buoyancy is the upward force on an object submerged in a fluid. Archimedes' principle states this force equals the weight of the fluid displaced. If buoyant force exceeds gravity, the object floats.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'water displacement' mean in the context of measuring density?

options.text: A method of measuring an object's volume by submerging it in water and recording the rise in water level
Removing water from a container to make room for a solid
Measuring how quickly water flows around an object
Weighing an object underwater to find its mass

quiz_questions.explanation: Water displacement measures the volume of an irregular object. When submerged, the object pushes aside (displaces) a volume of water equal to its own volume. The change in water level in a graduated cylinder gives the volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'specific gravity' mean?

options.text: The ratio of a substance's density to the density of water — it is dimensionless (no units)
The gravitational pull on a specific substance
The mass of a substance at a specific temperature
The density of a substance measured in space (zero gravity)

quiz_questions.explanation: Specific gravity = density of substance / density of water. Since it is a ratio of the same units, it is dimensionless. A specific gravity greater than 1 means the substance sinks in water; less than 1 means it floats.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'graduated cylinder' refer to?

options.text: A tall, narrow container with volume markings used to precisely measure liquid volume
A cylinder used to measure mass of solids
A device that heats liquids to their boiling point
A tube used to measure the density of gases

quiz_questions.explanation: A graduated cylinder has volume markings (graduations) along its side and is used to measure liquid volumes precisely. Read the volume at the bottom of the meniscus (the curved surface of the liquid).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'meniscus' mean?

options.text: The curved surface of a liquid in a container, caused by the interaction between the liquid and the container walls
The flat surface of a liquid at rest
The bubbles formed when a liquid boils
The steam rising from a heated liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: The meniscus is the curve at the top of a liquid column. Water forms a concave (dip down) meniscus in glass because water adheres to glass. Always read the volume at the bottom of a concave meniscus.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'mass' in chemistry?

options.text: The amount of matter in an object, measured in grams or kilograms
The space an object occupies, measured in liters
The force of gravity acting on an object
The density of an object times its temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass is the quantity of matter in an object. It is constant regardless of location (unlike weight, which depends on gravity). Mass is measured using a balance and reported in grams (g) or kilograms (kg).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'volume' mean in the context of density?

options.text: The amount of three-dimensional space an object or substance occupies
The weight of a substance at sea level
The loudness of a sound produced by a substance
The temperature at which a substance changes state

quiz_questions.explanation: Volume is the space occupied by matter. It is measured in mL, L, or cm³. For regular shapes, volume = length × width × height. For irregular shapes, water displacement is used.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Archimedes' Principle' state?

options.text: An object submerged in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced
All objects sink in water unless they contain air
Density determines the color of a substance
Mass and volume are always proportional to temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: the buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of the displaced fluid. If this buoyant force exceeds the object's weight, the object floats. This principle allows us to use water displacement to measure volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for a substance to be 'neutrally buoyant'?

options.text: The substance's density equals the fluid's density, so it neither floats nor sinks
The substance has zero mass in the fluid
The substance bounces between the surface and the bottom
The substance dissolves completely in the fluid

quiz_questions.explanation: Neutral buoyancy occurs when an object's density exactly matches the surrounding fluid's density. The buoyant force equals gravity, so the object remains suspended at whatever depth it is placed. Submarines and scuba divers use this principle.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the mass of a rock as 150 g. She places it in a graduated cylinder that contains 50 mL of water, and the water level rises to 80 mL. What is the density of the rock?

options.text: 5.0 g/mL — the rock's volume is 30 mL (80 − 50), and 150/30 = 5.0
3.0 g/mL — divide mass by final water level (150/50)
1.875 g/mL — divide mass by total volume (150/80)
2.0 g/mL — subtract water volume from mass (150 − 50 = 100, 150/100)

quiz_questions.explanation: The rock's volume equals the water displaced: 80 mL − 50 mL = 30 mL. Density = mass/volume = 150 g / 30 mL = 5.0 g/mL. Common errors include dividing by the final reading or the initial reading instead of the difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cruise ship made of steel (density = 7.8 g/cm³) floats on water. If steel is much denser than water, why doesn't the ship sink?

options.text: The ship's hull encloses a large volume of air, making the overall average density less than water
The ship's steel becomes less dense when shaped into a hull
Salt water is denser than steel, so all ships float in the ocean
The engines push the ship upward to keep it from sinking

quiz_questions.explanation: Although steel is denser than water, the ship's hull is shaped to enclose vast volumes of air. The total mass of the ship (steel + air + cargo) divided by the total volume is less than 1 g/mL, so the ship's overall density allows it to float.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chef makes a layered drink by pouring honey, water, and oil into a glass. The honey settles at the bottom, water in the middle, and oil on top. What does this demonstrate?

options.text: Liquids layer by density — honey is densest (bottom), water is in the middle, oil is least dense (top)
Lighter colors always float on top of darker ones
The liquids are arranged by the order they were poured
Viscosity determines which liquid is on top

quiz_questions.explanation: When immiscible liquids are combined, they separate into layers based on density. Honey (~1.4 g/mL) is the densest and sinks. Water (1.0 g/mL) is less dense than honey but denser than oil (~0.9 g/mL), so it sits in the middle.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist has two metal cubes of the same size. Cube A has a mass of 27 g and Cube B has a mass of 216 g. She needs to determine which is aluminum (d = 2.7 g/cm³) and which is silver (d = 10.5 g/cm³). If each cube's volume is 10 cm³, which cube is which?

options.text: Cube A is aluminum (27/10 = 2.7 g/cm³) and Cube B is neither standard value — need to recheck mass
Cube A is silver and Cube B is aluminum
Both are aluminum because they are the same size
Cube A is aluminum (27/10 = 2.7 g/cm³) and Cube B has a density of 21.6 g/cm³ — denser than silver, possibly gold or tungsten

quiz_questions.explanation: Cube A: d = 27 g / 10 cm³ = 2.7 g/cm³, matching aluminum. Cube B: d = 216 g / 10 cm³ = 21.6 g/cm³, which does not match silver (10.5) — it is closer to platinum (21.4) or gold (19.3). Density calculations can reveal unexpected identities.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ice floats in liquid water, which is unusual for a solid-liquid pair of the same substance. How does this relate to density?

options.text: Ice is less dense (0.917 g/cm³) than liquid water (1.00 g/cm³) because its crystal structure has open spaces
Ice is denser than water but contains trapped air bubbles that make it float
Ice and water have the same density, so ice just barely floats
Water pushes ice upward due to surface tension, not density

quiz_questions.explanation: Water is one of the few substances whose solid form is less dense than its liquid. When water freezes, hydrogen bonds create an open hexagonal crystal structure with more space between molecules, reducing density to 0.917 g/cm³. This is why ice floats.

quiz_questions.question_text: A gold dealer receives a bar claimed to be pure gold (d = 19.3 g/cm³). She measures the mass as 500 g and the volume as 38 cm³. Is the bar likely pure gold?

options.text: No — the calculated density is 13.2 g/cm³, much lower than gold; the bar is likely a cheaper metal or alloy
Yes — 500 g is a reasonable mass for a gold bar
Yes — 38 cm³ is a reasonable volume for gold
Cannot determine — mass and volume alone cannot identify a substance

quiz_questions.explanation: Density = 500 g / 38 cm³ = 13.2 g/cm³. Pure gold has a density of 19.3 g/cm³, so this bar is significantly less dense. It could be lead (11.3) plated with gold, or a gold-copper alloy. Density is a reliable way to detect counterfeits.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hot air balloon rises because the air inside the balloon is heated. How does heating the air change its density?

options.text: Heating makes air particles move faster and spread apart, decreasing the density below that of the surrounding cooler air
Heating removes air molecules from inside the balloon
The flame adds lighter gases that replace the original air
Heating increases the air's density, which pushes the balloon upward

quiz_questions.explanation: Heating a gas increases particle speed and spacing, causing expansion. In an open balloon, some air escapes as it expands, reducing the mass inside while volume stays large. This lowers the overall density below the surrounding cool air, creating buoyancy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Dead Sea water has a density of about 1.24 g/mL. A swimmer who normally sinks in a freshwater pool finds they float easily in the Dead Sea. Why?

options.text: The human body's density (~1.06 g/mL) is less than the Dead Sea water, so the buoyant force exceeds gravity
The high salt content makes the swimmer lighter
The Dead Sea has stronger waves that push swimmers up
The warm temperature of the Dead Sea increases the swimmer's buoyancy

quiz_questions.explanation: The average human body has a density of about 1.06 g/mL. In freshwater (1.00 g/mL), this means we barely sink. In the Dead Sea (1.24 g/mL), the water is denser than the body, so the buoyant force is greater than the person's weight, and they float easily.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lab technician needs to separate a mixture of plastic beads: Bead A (d = 0.85 g/cm³), Bead B (d = 1.05 g/cm³), and Bead C (d = 1.35 g/cm³). She places them all in water. What happens?

options.text: Bead A floats (less dense than water); Beads B and C sink (denser than water)
All three float because they are plastic
All three sink because they are solid
Bead C floats and Bead A sinks

quiz_questions.explanation: Objects float if their density is less than the fluid they're in. Water's density is 1.00 g/cm³. Bead A (0.85) floats. Beads B (1.05) and C (1.35) are denser than water and sink. To further separate B and C, a denser liquid (e.g., salt water at 1.2 g/mL) could be used.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer designs a submarine that must operate at two depths: 100 m and 500 m. At 500 m, the water pressure compresses the sub's hull slightly, reducing its volume. If the sub's mass stays the same, what happens to its density, and how does this affect buoyancy?

options.text: Density increases (same mass, less volume), reducing buoyancy — the sub tends to sink further unless it compensates
Density decreases because water pressure pushes the sub upward
Density stays the same because the sub is a solid object
The sub floats more easily at 500 m because deeper water is denser

quiz_questions.explanation: If the hull compresses and volume decreases while mass stays the same, d = m/v increases. A denser submarine displaces less water relative to its weight, reducing buoyancy. Real submarines compensate by pumping water in or out of ballast tanks to adjust overall density.

quiz_questions.question_text: A homogeneous mixture is also called a:
quiz_questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout and is also known as a solution. The solute is evenly distributed in the solvent at the molecular level, so the mixture looks the same in every part.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is an example of a heterogeneous mixture?
quiz_questions.explanation: A salad is heterogeneous because you can see and separate its individual components (lettuce, tomatoes, croutons). Salt water, air, and vinegar are all examples of homogeneous mixtures (solutions).

quiz_questions.question_text: In a solution, the substance being dissolved is called the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The solute is the substance that dissolves (usually present in smaller amount). The solvent is the substance doing the dissolving (usually present in larger amount). In salt water, salt is the solute and water is the solvent.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a homogeneous mixture?
quiz_questions.explanation: Vinegar is a homogeneous mixture of acetic acid dissolved in water — it has a uniform appearance throughout. Trail mix is heterogeneous (visible parts), and sand/water and oil/water are heterogeneous (they separate into visible layers).

quiz_questions.question_text: What distinguishes a heterogeneous mixture from a homogeneous one?

options.text: The parts are visibly different and not uniformly distributed
It contains only one type of element
It has a uniform composition throughout
It can only exist as a liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: Heterogeneous mixtures have a non-uniform composition — you can see distinct regions or components (e.g., salad, trail mix, sand in water). Homogeneous mixtures look the same throughout.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the best technique for separating a homogeneous mixture like salt water?
quiz_questions.explanation: Distillation separates components by differences in boiling point — you boil the water away and collect it, leaving the salt behind. Filtration works for heterogeneous mixtures with visible solid particles, and magnets only work with magnetic materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: Blood is classified as what type of mixture?
quiz_questions.explanation: Blood contains red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets, and plasma, which can be separated by centrifugation. Although it may look uniform to the naked eye, at the microscopic level it is clearly heterogeneous.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of mixture has particles that eventually settle out over time?
quiz_questions.explanation: A suspension contains large particles that are dispersed but will eventually settle out due to gravity (like muddy water). Solutions have dissolved particles that never settle, and colloids have intermediate particles that stay dispersed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Milk, fog, and gelatin are all examples of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Colloids contain particles larger than those in solutions but smaller than suspensions. They remain dispersed (don't settle) and often scatter light (the Tyndall effect). Milk, fog, and gelatin are classic examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: The substance in a solution that is present in the largest amount is called the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The solvent is the dissolving medium, typically present in the greatest amount. Water is the most common solvent and is called the 'universal solvent' because of its ability to dissolve many substances.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of a 'mixture'?

options.text: A combination of two or more substances that are NOT chemically bonded and can be separated by physical means
Two or more elements bonded in a fixed ratio
A single type of atom arranged in a crystal structure
A chemical reaction that produces new substances

quiz_questions.explanation: A mixture is a physical combination of substances that retain their individual properties. Unlike compounds, the components are not chemically bonded, the ratio is variable, and they can be separated by physical means (filtration, distillation, etc.).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'homogeneous' mean?

options.text: Having a uniform composition throughout — every sample of the mixture has the same proportions
Made of particles that settle over time

quiz_questions.explanation: Homogeneous means 'same throughout.' In a homogeneous mixture (solution), the solute is evenly dispersed at the molecular level, so any sample taken from any part of the mixture will have the same composition.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'heterogeneous' mean?

options.text: Having a non-uniform composition — different regions have different proportions of components
Made of a single pure substance
Living organisms mixed with non-living matter

quiz_questions.explanation: Heterogeneous means 'different throughout.' The components are not evenly distributed and can often be distinguished visually. A sample from one part may differ from a sample taken from another part.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'colloid'?

options.text: A mixture with particles larger than a solution but smaller than a suspension, that stay dispersed and don't settle out
A pure substance with no dissolved particles
A chemical compound that dissolves easily in water
A mixture where all particles are individual atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: Colloids have particles sized 1-1,000 nm. They are small enough to stay dispersed (unlike suspensions) but large enough to scatter light (Tyndall effect, unlike solutions). Examples: milk, fog, smoke, mayonnaise, gelatin.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'suspension'?

options.text: A heterogeneous mixture with large visible particles that will eventually settle out due to gravity
A homogeneous mixture of two liquids
A pure substance in the liquid phase

quiz_questions.explanation: Suspensions contain particles larger than 1,000 nm that are visibly dispersed but settle out over time. Muddy water, Italian dressing, and snow globes are examples. They can be separated by filtration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Tyndall effect' mean?

options.text: The scattering of a light beam by particles in a colloid, making the beam visible
The settling of large particles in a suspension over time
The dissolution of a solute in a solvent
The separation of a mixture using heat

quiz_questions.explanation: The Tyndall effect is observed when light passes through a colloid — the beam becomes visible because particles scatter the light. It does not occur in true solutions (particles too small) or clear liquids. Think of car headlights visible in fog.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'solubility' mean?

options.text: The maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a given amount of solvent at a specific temperature
The speed at which a solute dissolves
The ability of a substance to separate from a mixture
The temperature at which a solid melts

quiz_questions.explanation: Solubility is a quantitative measure of how much solute can dissolve in a solvent at a given temperature. For example, about 36 g of NaCl dissolve in 100 mL of water at 25°C. Solubility generally increases with temperature for solids.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'filtration' mean?

options.text: A separation method that uses a porous barrier to separate solid particles from a liquid or gas
A method of combining two solutions together
Heating a liquid to separate it from dissolved solids
Dissolving a solid completely in a liquid

quiz_questions.explanation: Filtration passes a mixture through a filter (paper, cloth, membrane) that allows small particles and liquid to pass through while trapping larger solid particles. It works for heterogeneous mixtures and suspensions but not for solutions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'distillation' mean?

options.text: A separation technique that uses differences in boiling points — heating to vaporize one component, then cooling to collect it
Filtering a mixture through paper to remove solids
Adding a chemical to break down a compound
Freezing a solution to separate its components

quiz_questions.explanation: Distillation exploits boiling point differences. The mixture is heated until the component with the lower boiling point vaporizes, leaving the other behind. The vapor is then condensed and collected. This can separate solutions (e.g., salt water → pure water + salt).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'alloy' mean?

options.text: A homogeneous mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and a nonmetal), such as brass or steel
A heterogeneous mixture of different metals
A pure metal that has been melted and re-solidified
A chemical compound containing two metal elements

quiz_questions.explanation: An alloy is a solid solution of metals. Brass (copper + zinc), steel (iron + carbon), and bronze (copper + tin) are common examples. Alloys are homogeneous mixtures because the atoms are uniformly distributed throughout.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student shines a flashlight through a glass of lemonade (sugar water + lemon juice) and a glass of whole milk. The beam is invisible in the lemonade but clearly visible in the milk. What explains this difference?

options.text: Milk is a colloid with particles large enough to scatter light (Tyndall effect); lemonade is a true solution with particles too small to scatter light
Lemonade absorbs all the light, so none is scattered
Milk is darker in color, which makes the light beam visible
The flashlight is not bright enough to pass through lemonade

quiz_questions.explanation: The Tyndall effect occurs in colloids: milk contains fat globules (1-10 μm) that scatter light, making the beam visible. Lemonade is a true solution with dissolved sugar and citric acid molecules too small to scatter visible light.

quiz_questions.question_text: A gold ring is labeled '14 karat,' meaning it is 58.3% gold and 41.7% other metals (copper, silver). Is this a mixture, compound, or pure substance? What type?

options.text: Homogeneous mixture (alloy) — the metals are uniformly blended but not chemically bonded
Compound — the metals are chemically bonded in fixed proportions
Heterogeneous mixture — you can see separate gold and copper regions
Pure substance — 14K gold is still classified as pure gold

quiz_questions.explanation: 14K gold is an alloy — a solid solution of gold with copper, silver, and/or other metals. The metals are physically mixed at the atomic level (homogeneous) but not chemically bonded. The proportions can vary, unlike a compound with fixed ratios.

quiz_questions.question_text: A water treatment plant needs to remove sand, bacteria, and dissolved minerals from river water. What sequence of separation methods would be most effective?

options.text: Filtration (removes sand), then fine filtration or UV treatment (removes bacteria), then distillation or reverse osmosis (removes dissolved minerals)
Distillation alone can remove everything at once
Using a magnet, then freezing the water

quiz_questions.explanation: Different contaminants require different methods. Sand (large particles in a suspension) is removed by filtration. Bacteria (microscopic) need fine filtration or UV light. Dissolved minerals (solution) require distillation or reverse osmosis since they pass through regular filters.

quiz_questions.question_text: Italian salad dressing separates into an oil layer on top and a vinegar layer on the bottom after sitting. A student shakes it vigorously and says, 'Now it's a solution!' Is the student correct?

options.text: No — shaking creates a temporary suspension (or emulsion), but oil and vinegar are immiscible and will separate again
Yes — shaking dissolves the oil in the vinegar, creating a solution
Yes — the mixture is now homogeneous and will stay mixed permanently
No — shaking creates a compound by chemically bonding oil and vinegar

quiz_questions.explanation: Oil (nonpolar) and vinegar (polar) are immiscible — they don't dissolve in each other. Shaking temporarily disperses oil droplets in vinegar, but since they aren't truly dissolved, they will separate again. An emulsifier (like egg yolk in mayonnaise) can stabilize such mixtures.
